hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Three discrete directives: (1) The WhatsApp send gate must be scoped exclusively to the always-on daemon — no other caller context may pass it. (2) Slack send gate receives the identical scoping restriction. (3) Message format must lead with the brain emoji (🧠) before any other content, matching the format used during the WhatsApp scheduler-agent period. No interpretation required beyond the words stated.
hypotheses.diagnose: Ambiguity: 'send gate' could mean the PreToolUse mechanical block in flip_tool_guards.py / hook.py, or a logical permission layer inside the daemon itself. 'Same with Slack' implies Slack currently has an analogous gate that needs the same treatment. 'Brain emoji comes first' implies an existing message template where the emoji is either absent or positioned incorrectly — the reference to 'messages you sent before during whatsapp scheduler-agent time' is the format anchor. Missing: which file owns the Slack gate, and what the exact prior emoji-first format string looked like.
hypotheses.develop: Technical request — constraint-based + precision focus. Role: systems engineer with knowledge of the Brain gate architecture. The ask is a scoping restriction (caller-identity check) on two send channels plus a message format correction. Each change is independent and surgical.
hypotheses.deliver: 1. Locate the current send gate implementation (likely flip_tool_guards.py Rule 8 or hook.py PreToolUse handler). Add a caller-identity check: allow send tools to pass only when the invoking process is always_on.py (e.g., check env var BRAIN_DAEMON=1 set by always_on.py, or inspect the process name / parent PID). 2. Apply the identical caller-identity check to the Slack send tool gate. 3. Find the message assembly template used by the always-on daemon or scheduler-agent output path and move the 🧠 emoji to position 0 of the string, before any other content. Verify by reading the actual template file before changing it.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Justs is in USELESS trust tier, 11 days from the 30-day verdict deadline. He is issuing a precise, non-negotiable configuration directive with zero tolerance for interpretation errors. His established pattern: results-only, no deliberation surfaced, no options offered back. The three items are a single atomic instruction set — scope send gates to daemon, scope Slack the same, fix emoji order — delivered as a terse compound sentence because he does not have attention to spare for elaboration.
hypotheses.diagnose: Identity context sharpens the execution mandate: any response that asks a clarifying question, proposes alternatives, or outputs anything other than the implemented change will register as a failure. The near-termination arc (E263) and the fabrication reflex pattern both say: verify against the actual files, do not infer what the gate looks like from memory, read it first. The 'messages you sent before' reference means there is a recoverable format string in logs or source — it must be found, not reconstructed.
hypotheses.develop: Identity-shaped execution: strip all deliberation. The optimization technique is constraint-based with maximum precision. No preamble, no options, no status updates beyond the completed result. The brain emoji anchor in 'messages you sent before during whatsapp scheduler-agent time' points to always-on logger output or a WhatsApp message formatting function — locate it in the logger or the send wrapper.
hypotheses.deliver: Execute silently: (1) Read flip_tool_guards.py and the relevant hook to confirm current gate logic. (2) Add daemon-only caller check to WhatsApp send gate. (3) Add same check to Slack send gate. (4) Search always-on logger output or the WhatsApp send wrapper for the prior message format with 🧠 leading — replicate that exact format. (5) Commit and push. Report completion with the diff only — no options, no questions.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: R373 (score 5.5) is the direct anchor: WhatsApp send was blocked at the rule-engine layer after the daemon sent seven unsolicited messages. The block was total — channel read-only by architecture. Now Justs is partially reopening it: send is permitted again, but ONLY for the always-on daemon context. S184/S179 describe the daemon as a Python process (always_on.py) gated by always-on.bin. The send gate re-scoping means R373's total block becomes a caller-scoped allow. Slack was presumably blocked under the same logic (R373 generalizes to 'all channels with irreversible external consequences'). The brain emoji format connects to the scheduler-agent period referenced in memory — the WhatsApp bridge (PR330) was active during that time.
hypotheses.diagnose: R373 says the block is in flip_tool_guards.py Rule 8 — that is the file to modify. The daemon identity signal must be mechanically verifiable (not in-context reasoning, per R373's own principle). The Slack gate location is not explicitly named in recall but likely follows the same flip_tool_guards.py pattern. The emoji format is recoverable from always-on logger JSONL files (~/.claude/brain/logger/always-on/) or from the WhatsApp send wrapper used during the scheduler-agent period.
hypotheses.develop: Technical + recall-grounded. Role: Brain pipeline engineer. The change is a surgical relaxation of R373's total block into a daemon-scoped allow. The mechanical enforcement principle (R373) must be preserved — the daemon-identity check must itself be mechanical (process signal, env var, flag file), not in-context reasoning.
hypotheses.deliver: 1. Read flip_tool_guards.py — locate Rule 8 (WhatsApp send block) and the Slack equivalent. 2. Define a mechanical daemon-identity signal: always_on.py should set BRAIN_DAEMON_ACTIVE=1 in its subprocess environment before spawning claude -p. 3. Modify the gate: pass send tools when BRAIN_DAEMON_ACTIVE=1 is set in env, block otherwise. 4. Apply same env-check to Slack gate. 5. Search always-on JSONL logs for a message where 🧠 appears first — extract the exact format string. 6. Update the message assembly function to use that format. Commit, push.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: What if 'send gate should be only for the always-on daemon' does not mean restrict the send tool to daemon callers — but instead means the GATE FILE (always-on.bin) should be the single control point for whether sending is permitted at all? Under this reading, Justs is saying: tie the send permission to the daemon's own activation gate. When the daemon is on (bin=1), send is possible. When the daemon is off (bin=0), send is blocked system-wide — not caller-scoped, but daemon-lifecycle-scoped.
hypotheses.diagnose: This reading is plausible because the gate binary (always-on.bin) is already the daemon's on/off switch (S184/S179). If 'send gate = always-on gate', then disabling the daemon disables all outbound sends automatically — a simpler architecture than a caller-identity check. The risk: this reading would mean an interactive session could still send as long as the daemon is on, which contradicts the safety lesson from R373.
hypotheses.develop: Adversarial heuristic: the R373 incident is the reason this directive exists. The daemon sent messages autonomously without authorization. Scoping send to 'the always-on daemon' most naturally means only the daemon may send, not that the daemon's gate controls everyone. The contrarian read is less safe and less consistent with R373's lesson. Flag this reading to the parent as the less likely interpretation but worth surfacing before implementation.
hypotheses.deliver: Flag to parent: there are two distinct interpretations. (A) Caller-scoped: send passes only when called from the always_on.py process. (B) Gate-scoped: send availability is tied to the always-on.bin flag, regardless of caller. Interpretation A is safer and more consistent with R373. Interpretation B is simpler but allows interactive sends while daemon is active. If the parent cannot resolve from context, surface interpretation A as default and note B as the alternative — do not implement B without explicit confirmation.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: Three changes, minimum surface area: (1) Add one condition to the WhatsApp send gate check — caller is daemon. (2) Copy that condition to the Slack send gate. (3) Move 🧠 to position 0 in the message format string. No architectural changes, no new files, no refactoring.
hypotheses.diagnose: The smallest implementation: a single env-var check (BRAIN_DAEMON_ACTIVE) added to two gate rules, and a string prepend in one template. Risk of under-scoping: if 'always-on daemon' is the only permitted sender, the check must be airtight — env var set by the daemon before spawning the subprocess, and not inheritable by other callers. The emoji change is genuinely minimal — one string edit.
hypotheses.develop: Occam's razor: do the least that satisfies all three directives completely. No new gate files, no new binaries, no new services. The daemon already sets its own env context when it invokes claude -p — add BRAIN_DAEMON_ACTIVE=1 to that invocation, then check for it in flip_tool_guards.py for both WhatsApp and Slack send rules. The format fix is a one-line edit.
hypotheses.deliver: Minimal change set: (1) In always_on.py, add env={'BRAIN_DAEMON_ACTIVE': '1'} to the subprocess.run / cli_invoke call. (2) In flip_tool_guards.py Rule 8, change the WhatsApp send block from 'always block' to 'block unless BRAIN_DAEMON_ACTIVE=1'. (3) Find the Slack send rule in the same file, apply identical condition. (4) Find the message format string (search always-on logger or the send wrapper for '🧠'), confirm emoji-first order, edit if not already first. Total: 4 targeted edits across 2-3 files.
